title: Data Quality Filters for SFT
concept: Deduplicate and filter instruction sets to remove robotic, repetitive responses, as the model will quickly copy these stylistic habits.

exercises.prompt: What is the primary goal of Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT)?

options.text: To make the model run faster on mobile devices.
To teach a raw model how to follow instructions and converse.
To translate the model's code into another programming language.

exercises.explain: SFT stands for Supervised Fine-Tuning, where we teach a pre-trained model how to behave like a helpful assistant using prompt-and-response examples.

cards.title: Stop the AI Repetitions!
cards.body: Have you ever talked to an AI that kept repeating the exact same polite intro, like 'As a helpful assistant...' over and over? That happens because of bad training data! Let's learn how we clean datasets to keep AI sounding natural.

cards.title: What is SFT Data Filtering?
cards.body: Data Quality Filtering for SFT is the process of cleaning up instruction datasets. We remove repetitive prompt-response pairs, robotic text, and duplicate examples. If we don't, the AI will quickly copy these bad habits during training.

cards.title: The Repetitive Parrot
cards.body: Imagine training a parrot by repeating the exact same phrase ten times a day. Instead of learning to speak freely, the parrot will only squawk that one repetitive phrase. Deduplication is like giving the parrot a colorful variety of unique phrases so it learns to be a natural speaker instead of a broken record!

cards.title: Cleaning the Dataset
cards.body:
Before filtering, a dataset might contain 50 questions that all start with: 'As an AI assistant, I am happy to help you with...'
After filtering, we remove these repetitive preambles. This ensures the model learns to answer directly and naturally instead of sounding like a robotic machine.

cards.title: Myth: More Data is Always Better
cards.body: People often think that the larger the dataset, the smarter the AI will be. In reality, a smaller dataset of high-quality, diverse, and clean text trains a much better-behaved model than a massive dataset filled with repetitive, robotic clutter!

cards.title: Clean vs. Cluttered Data
cards.body: Let's compare a messy, unfiltered dataset with a clean, filtered one. Notice how filtering creates diversity and natural language.

left.text: Has 1,000 examples starting with: 'As an AI, I...' and lots of duplicate prompts.

right.text: Has unique, varied prompt styles and direct, human-like answers.

exercises.prompt: What happens if you train an AI on a dataset filled with robotic, repetitive assistant responses?

options.text: The model will forget how to speak English entirely.
The model will quickly copy these stylistic habits and sound repetitive.
The model will automatically delete the repetitive data itself.
The model's file size will shrink to half its original size.

exercises.explain: Fine-tuning models are highly sensitive to style. If the training data contains repetitive phrases, the model will quickly learn to copy those exact patterns.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Deduplication helps ensure the AI doesn't get stuck repeating the same phrases.
exercises.explain: Deduplication removes identical or highly similar prompt-response pairs, ensuring the model is exposed to a diverse range of language patterns.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank to complete the statement about SFT data cleaning.
exercises.sentence: Removing repetitive and near-identical training examples from your dataset is called SFT dataset ____________.
exercises.explain: Deduplication removes identical or highly similar examples so the model doesn't overlearn repetitive phrasing.

exercises.prompt: Which of these AI responses shows the result of good data quality filtering?

options.text: Response A: 'As an AI language model, I am programmed to tell you that the capital of France is Paris.'
Response B: 'The capital of France is Paris.'
Response C: 'Dear valued user, I am an automated program. Paris is the capital of France.'

exercises.explain: Robotic templates ('As an AI, I am unable to...') should be cleaned out. Natural, direct answers make for a much better user experience.

exercises.prompt: Why do we apply strict data quality filters to our instruction datasets before Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT)?

options.text: It helps the model memorize the training dataset perfectly word-for-word.
It prevents the model from picking up annoying, repetitive, and robotic habits.
It guarantees the model will run faster during training.
It removes the need to train on any text data at all.

exercises.explain: Without strict quality filters, LLMs are highly prone to copying repetitive templates, greeting styles, or robotic phrasing. Filtering out low-quality and duplicate samples is essential to make the final model sound natural.
